section: Evolution of microstructure, texture and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling

context_update.summary: This chunk contains the abstract and introduction of the paper. The abstract reports key findings from a systematic investigation of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) steel during cold rolling up to 90% thickness reduction. The stacking fault energy (SFE) was estimated as ~108 mJ/m². Yield strength exceeded 2.0 GPa after 90% reduction due to microstructure refinement. Deformation substructures evolve in the sequence: Taylor lattice, microbands, nano-twins, shear bands, and dislocation cells, indicating a dislocation glide mode transition from planar to wavy slip. Deformation twinning occurred despite the high SFE, attributed to short-range order and local stress concentration. The rolling texture was weak and showed a transition from copper-type to brass-type after 50% reduction, with dislocation planar glide and twinning playing important roles. The introduction provides background on high Mn-Al-C steels, emphasizing the lack of systematic cold rolling studies and stating the research objective. Subsequent chunks are expected to detail experimental methods, microstructural evolution at different reductions, mechanical properties, and texture analysis. Remember the material system is Fe-30Mn-11Al-1.2C, and look for sample states labeled by reduction percentages, as well as figures/tables for substructures and texture.
key_variables: substructure sequence: Taylor lattice, microbands, nano-twins, shear bands, dislocation cells
texture transition from copper-type to brass-type after 50% reduction
properties: yield strength exceeding 2.0 GPa after 90% reduction
mechanisms: deformation twinning attributed to SRO and local stress concentration
dislocation planar glide and twinning influence texture evolution

context_update.summary: This chunk describes the material processing (vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1250°C/12 h, hot forging, solution treatment at 1100°C/1 h, and cold rolling to 10–90% reduction), experimental procedures (tensile testing, OM, EBSD, TEM, XRD), initial mechanical properties, and microstructure evolution. The as-quenched steel had YS ≈570 MPa, UTS ≈830 MPa, UE >75%, and a microstructure of austenite with nano κ'-carbides, annealing twins (~70 µm grain), and random texture. Cold rolling increased yield strength to ~2.0 GPa (3.5×) while reducing uniform elongation. Microstructure evolution was divided into two stages: Stage I (10–50%) dominated by Taylor lattice (planar slip) with gradual refinement of slip band spacing; Stage II (70–90%) characterized by shear bands, elongated grains, and formation of microbands, twins, and dislocation cells. TEM revealed slip band spacing (231 nm at 30%, 64 nm at 50%), microband width (166 nm), twin thickness (5.1 nm at 50%, 19.2 nm at 70%), and dislocation tangles (0.49 µm). Important figures: Fig. 1 (stress-strain curves), Fig. 2 (initial microstructure), Fig. 3 (OM stages), Fig. 4–6 (TEM substructures). Subsequent chunks should address texture evolution and further microstructure-property relationships.
microstructure: Taylor lattice (planar slip) in stage I
shear bands and elongated grains in stage II
deformation twins 5.1 nm at 50%, 19.2 nm at 70%
properties: YS increases to ~2.0 GPa at 90% reduction
work hardening capability lost at high reductions
mechanisms: planar dislocation glide leading to Taylor lattice
spinodal decomposition forming κ'-carbides due to high Al and C
possible cross slip at slip band intersections
microband and deformation twin formation accommodating deformation
figures_or_tables: Fig. 6 (TEM shear bands, twins, dislocation tangles at 70%)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers texture evolution (Section 3.3) and the beginning of the Discussion (Section 4.1) on microstructure-property relationships. The initial texture is random. Key findings from ODF analysis: α-fiber (Brass) increases monotonically to 90% reduction, with its maximum shifting from Ψ=45° to 20° and Brass scattering toward Goss; τ-fiber shows Cu peak at 50% then decreasing, while Goss increases and Φ shifts higher; a weak CuT component appears at 70% and evolves; γ-fiber remains weak with slight strengthening at 90% at the E position; β-fiber Cu increases then decreases, S has a delayed decrease at 70%, and Goss increases, with a continuous shift from Brass toward Goss along β-fiber. The Discussion links deformation stress levels to substructure evolution: <1.2 GPa refines Taylor lattice spacing via planar glide; ~1.5 GPa forms microbands (GND boundaries) and nano-twins, despite high critical twinning stress (~1.48 GPa calculated vs. ~0.75 GPa resolved) attributed to SRO and local stress concentration; ~1.8 GPa generates microshear bands; ~2.1 GPa forms dislocation cells with cross slip. The substructure sequence is Taylor lattice → microbands → twins → shear bands → dislocation tangles. Strengthening mechanisms: Taylor lattice strength from long-range coplanar slip-band interaction (σTL=K·M·G·b/D); later substructures strengthen via grain-boundary Hall-Petch effects, achieving ~2.0 GPa yield strength. Important figures/tables: Fig. 7 (schematic of texture components), Table 1 (component definitions), Fig. 8 (ODF sections), Fig. 9(a-f) (fiber intensity plots); Discussion references Fig. 4,5,6.
key_variables: Cu peak at 50% and then decrease
SRO and local stress concentration promote twinning
microstructure: ODF fiber development: α, τ, γ, β
properties: yield strength ~2.0 GPa realized through substructure strengthening
mechanisms: planar glide and glide plane softening
texture transition from Cu-type to Brass-type after 50%

context_update.summary: Section 4.2 introduces two key observations on texture evolution in cold-rolled Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C steel. First, the overall texture intensity is weaker compared to copper alloys and nickel. Second, a transition from copper-type to brass-type texture occurs at 50% reduction, marked by a gradual reduction in Cu and S components. The ODF sections in Fig. 8 and fiber evolution data in Fig. 9 (including α-, τ-, γ-, β-fibers) document these changes. No additional mechanisms or property relations are discussed in this chunk; the transition is merely identified. The chunk ends mid-sentence, but the transition threshold and component trends are clear. Future sections will likely elaborate on deformation mechanisms causing the copper-to-brass texture transition.
key_variables: texture intensity weakness vs. copper alloys and nickel
texture transition from copper-type to brass-type at 50% reduction
gradual decrease of Cu and S components

context_update.summary: This chunk establishes the relationship between microstructure and texture evolution in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C steel. It confirms that dislocation multiple planar glide dominates despite some cross slip at higher reductions. The weak overall texture is attributed to planar glide and scattering by microbands, shear bands and twins. The copper-to-brass texture transition after 50% reduction is linked to two mechanisms: (i) dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloys) and (ii) deformation twinning (as in Cu–Zn and TWIP steels). Twinning is evidenced indirectly by CuT development and slight γ-fiber strengthening, with nano-scale twins forming T-M lamellae. The Cu/CuT shift reflects latent hardening, and microshear bands correlate with a rapid G component increase. The section ends with the Conclusions header, indicating that the key findings are about to be listed. Future chunks should capture the enumerated conclusions and any remaining property–structure correlations. Important figures are Fig. 3(c-e), Fig. 5(d), Fig. 6(a-c), and Fig. 9(b-c).
mechanisms: Texture weakening via planar glide and substructure scattering
Copper-to-brass transition via planar slip in absence of twins
Copper-to-brass transition via twinning: Cu→CuT→G/B, with T-M lamella abnormal rotation and microshear band formation
Latent hardening reflected by Cu/CuT shift

Material systems: Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) low-density steel; Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) steel; Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) low-density austenitic steel; Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C austenitic low-density steel; Cu–Ni; Ni–Cr; Cu–Mn; L1₂ ordered intermetallic alloys
Sample aliases: as-quenched; 10% cold-rolled reduction; 30% cold-rolled reduction; 50% cold-rolled reduction; 70% cold-rolled reduction; 90% cold-rolled reduction; 10% cold-rolled; 30% cold-rolled
Processing route: cold rolling; vacuum induction melting; soaking at 1250°C for 12 h; hot-forging (finish ~900°C); solution treatment at 1100°C for 1 h; cold rolling to 10–90% reduction; cold rolling up to 90% reduction; hot forging
Key variables: cold rolling reduction up to 90%; SFE ~108 mJ/m²; yield strength >2.0 GPa; substructure sequence: Taylor lattice, microbands, nano-twins, shear bands, dislocation cells; planar to wavy glide transition; texture transition from copper-type to brass-type after 50% reduction; cold rolling reduction 10%–90%; yield strength up to ~2.0 GPa
Microstructure: Taylor lattice; microbands; nano-twins; shear bands; dislocation cells; weak rolling texture; copper-type to brass-type after 50% reduction; austenite + nano κ'-carbides (as-quenched)
Properties: yield strength exceeding 2.0 GPa after 90% reduction; YS ≈570 MPa, UTS ≈830 MPa, UE >75% (as-quenched); YS increases to ~2.0 GPa at 90% reduction; work hardening capability lost at high reductions; yield strength ~2.0 GPa realized through substructure strengthening
